products.name: EchoLake Bass Spinning Rod 6'9" Medium

Featuring a sensitive graphite blank, stainless guides, and a split-grip EVA handle, it is rated for 6–12 lb line and 1/4–3/4 oz lures.

products.description: Designed for anglers who need extra backbone, the EchoLake Bass Spinning Rod 7'0" Medium-Heavy handles 8–17 lb line and 3/8–1 oz lures. Perfect for tubes, Carolina rigs, and light swimbaits, it features a fast-action blank, durable guides, and a comfortable split-grip handle.

Infused with salt and scent, these baits excel for redfish, speckled trout, and flounder when rigged on 1/8–1/4 oz jig heads or weighted swimbait hooks.

blog_posts.title: Top 5 lures for spring bass
blog_posts.excerpt: From squarebills to flat-sides, these five lures cover every key spring bass scenario.
blog_posts.content_html:
Top 5 lures for spring bass

Spring is one of the best times of the year to catch big bass, but the conditions change quickly. Water temps, water clarity, and where bass are in the spawn cycle all affect which lure is best. Below are my top 5 confidence baits for spring bass, along with links so you can check each one out.

  1. 1. RiverRattler Squarebill Crankbait 1.5

    The RiverRattler Squarebill Crankbait 1.5 shines in 2–5 feet of water around wood and rock. It hunts hard and deflects well, making it my go-to for beating the bank on warming afternoons.

    View Product: RiverRattler Squarebill Crankbait 1.5

  2. 2. ShadFlash Lipless Crankbait 1/2 oz

    When bass are roaming grass flats or staging off points, a lipless crankbait lets you cover water fast. The ShadFlash Lipless Crankbait 1/2 oz is perfect for yo-yo retrieves over submerged vegetation.

    View Product: ShadFlash Lipless Crankbait 1/2 oz

  3. 3. CrawChaser Flat-Side Crankbait

    In cold, clear water, subtle action usually beats a wide wobble. The CrawChaser Flat-Side Crankbait is deadly on rocky banks and transition areas during late winter and early spring.

    View Product: CrawChaser Flat-Side Crankbait

  4. 4. DeepCreep Medium Diver Crankbait

    When fish pull a little deeper to secondary points or channel swings, the DeepCreep Medium Diver Crankbait keeps your bait in the 8–12 ft zone where those bigger prespawn females often sit.

    View Product: DeepCreep Medium Diver Crankbait

  5. 5. RiverRattler Squarebill Crankbait 2.5

    If they want a bigger meal or the water is stained, upsize to the RiverRattler Squarebill Crankbait 2.5. It pushes more water, calls fish from farther away, and is a great choice when targeting bigger bites.

    View Product: RiverRattler Squarebill Crankbait 2.5

With these five lures, you can cover just about every spring bass situation—from cold fronts to the first real warming trend of the year.

blog_posts.title: Winter trout fishing guide: lures, line, and tactics
blog_posts.excerpt: Dial in your winter trout approach with subtle spoons, light fluorocarbon, and precise presentations.
blog_posts.content_html:
Winter trout fishing guide: lures, line, and tactics

Winter trout can be finicky, but with the right combination of lure and line, you can still have consistent days on the water. Cold water slows trout metabolism, so focus on smaller profiles, slower retrieves, and stealthy line choices.

Start with the right winter spoon

My first recommendation for cold-water trout is a compact spoon with a slow, fluttering wobble. The WinterGlow Trout Spoon 1/8 oz was designed specifically for this situation and has become a staple for my winter trips.

View WinterGlow Trout Spoon 1/8 oz

Cast it slightly upstream and let it sink near bottom, then lift the rod tip just enough to make the spoon wobble and fall again. Most bites come on the fall.

Line choice for spooky winter trout

In clear winter water, line visibility matters. A dedicated trout fluorocarbon like ClearCreek Trout Fluorocarbon Line in 4–6 lb test keeps your presentation natural while offering great abrasion resistance around rocks and ice shelves.

Spool a light spinning reel with the fluorocarbon or use it as a long leader connected to a thin braided main line.

Location and retrieve tips

  • Focus on deeper pools and slow current seams.
  • Fish during the warmest part of the day when water temperatures bump up a degree or two.
  • Slow everything down—retrieves, hooksets, and movements on the bank.

Combine a small, subtle spoon with light line and stealthy boat or bank positioning, and you'll be surprised how good winter trout fishing can be.

blog_posts.title: Build a saltwater starter kit under $250
blog_posts.excerpt: One rod, one reel, and three lure styles that cover most inshore saltwater situations—without breaking the bank.
blog_posts.content_html:
Build a saltwater starter kit under $250

If you're just getting into inshore saltwater fishing, you don't need a huge pile of gear. With the right rod, reel, and a few lure categories, you can cover most redfish, speckled trout, and flounder situations for less than $250.

Rod: a versatile 7' medium inshore spinning rod

Start with a 7' medium-power spinning rod rated for 8–17 lb line. The TideRunner Inshore Spinning Rod 7'0" Medium is a budget-friendly option that still has the backbone to handle big redfish.

View TideRunner Inshore Spinning Rod 7'0" Medium

Reel: 3000-size saltwater spinning reel

Pair that rod with a corrosion-resistant spinning reel like the TideRunner 3000 Saltwater Spinning Reel. Its sealed drag and aluminum body make it ideal for inshore use.

View TideRunner 3000 Saltwater Spinning Reel

Lures: soft plastics, jigs, and spoons

With three lure types, you can cover shallow flats, channels, and surf:

  • Soft plastics: A 4" paddle-tail like the SaltShad Paddle Tail 4" Soft Plastics (5-pack) works on jig heads or swimbait hooks.
    View SaltShad Paddle Tail 4" Soft Plastics
  • Jigs: A 1/2 oz jig such as the ReefFlash Inshore Jig 1/2 oz lets you probe deeper channels and structure.
    View ReefFlash Inshore Jig 1/2 oz
  • Spoons: For long casts from shore or jetties, a SurfGlide Casting Spoon 1 oz is tough to beat.
    View SurfGlide Casting Spoon 1 oz

Round out the setup with a 300-yard spool of 20–30 lb braid and a fluorocarbon leader, and you're ready for most inshore scenarios—still staying under a $250 budget.

blog_posts.title: Braided line 101: choosing strength and spool size
blog_posts.excerpt: How to pick the right pound test and 300-yard spool for bass, pike, and saltwater inshore fishing.
blog_posts.content_html:
Braided line 101: choosing strength and spool size

Braided line offers incredible sensitivity and strength, but choosing the right pound test and spool length can be confusing. Here’s how to dial it in for your style of fishing.

Why a 300-yard spool makes sense

For most baitcasting and spinning setups, a 300-yard spool of braid lets you fully spool one reel and top off a second. It's also usually the best value per yard.

40 lb braid for heavy cover bass and pike

If you flip grass, throw frogs, or fish around wood, a 40 lb class braid is ideal. A spool like PowerWeave Braided Line 300 yd - 40 lb strikes a balance between strength and castability.

View PowerWeave Braided Line 300 yd - 40 lb

65 lb braid for maximum stopping power

When you’re punching matted vegetation or targeting big catfish and stripers, step up to a 65 lb braid. PowerWeave Max Braided Line 300 yd - 65 lb is built for winching fish out of the nastiest cover.

View PowerWeave Max Braided Line 300 yd - 65 lb

Pair your braid with an appropriate leader (or none in dirty water), and you’ll get more solid hooksets and fewer break-offs.

blog_posts.title: How to choose the right waders using a size chart
blog_posts.excerpt: Use your waist and inseam measurements to pick the perfect stockingfoot wader size, like Medium Short for a 34" waist and 31" inseam.
blog_posts.content_html:
How to choose the right waders using a size chart

Nothing ruins a day of trout fishing faster than ill-fitting waders. The good news is that if you know your waist and inseam, you can quickly match them to the right size on a wader size chart.

Step 1: Take accurate measurements

  • Waist: Measure around your natural waist over the clothing you typically wear fishing.
  • Inseam: Measure from your crotch to the floor (without shoes).

Step 2: Match to the size chart

For example, if your waist is around 34" and your inseam is about 31", most charts recommend a Medium Short wader size. On our size guide, Medium Short is built for approximately 32–36" waist and 29–31" inseam.

Step 3: Choose a quality breathable wader

A popular option is the RiverGuard Breathable Stockingfoot Waders, which come in sizes including Small Short, Medium Short, Medium Regular, and Large Regular.

View RiverGuard Breathable Stockingfoot Waders

Always check the size guide link on the product page before purchasing, and remember to account for layering if you fish in cold weather.

blog_posts.title: Organize your tackle: best 3- and 4-tray boxes
blog_posts.excerpt: Keep your crankbaits, jigs, and terminal tackle dialed in with 3- and 4-tray boxes that fit boat or bank fishing.
blog_posts.content_html:
Organize your tackle: best 3- and 4-tray boxes

If you’ve ever wasted time digging through a messy pile of baits, you know how important a good tackle box is. Multi-tray hard boxes are still hard to beat for anglers who carry a mix of lures and terminal tackle.

Compact option: 3-tray box

The LakePro 3-Tray Tackle Box is ideal for bank anglers or anyone who wants a lighter kit. Three cantilever trays offer up to 24 adjustable compartments.

View LakePro 3-Tray Tackle Box

Maximum storage: 4-tray deep box

If you carry lots of crankbaits and soft plastics, step up to the LakePro 4-Tray Deep Tackle Box. With four trays and up to 36 compartments, it’s a great boat box.

View LakePro 4-Tray Deep Tackle Box

Whichever style you choose, label your trays by category (crankbaits, terminal, jigs, etc.) so you can grab what you need at a glance.

blog_posts.content_html:

    Shop RiverRattler Squarebill Crankbait 2.5

    Perfect for 8–12 ft secondary points and channel swings in cooler water.

blog_posts.title: Winter jigging for inshore redfish and trout
blog_posts.excerpt: Slow down with jigs and soft plastics to catch more redfish and speckled trout in cold water.
blog_posts.content_html:
Winter jigging for inshore redfish and trout

When water temperatures drop, inshore fish like redfish and speckled trout slide into deeper holes and channels. Jigs and soft plastics are perfect tools for picking apart these areas slowly.

Rod and reel setup

A 7' medium inshore spinning rod like the TideRunner Inshore Spinning Rod 7'0" Medium paired with a TideRunner 3000 Saltwater Spinning Reel gives you enough sensitivity and power for winter jigging.

View TideRunner Inshore Spinning Rod | View TideRunner 3000 Reel

Jigs and soft plastics

Match a 1/2 oz ReefFlash Inshore Jig 1/2 oz with a 4" SaltShad Paddle Tail for a compact profile that still gets down in current.

View ReefFlash Inshore Jig 1/2 oz | View SaltShad Paddle Tail 4" Soft Plastics

Fish slowly with small hops just off bottom, and watch your line closely—many winter bites are subtle ticks.

blog_posts.title: Topwater vs crankbait: when to choose each for bass
blog_posts.excerpt: Decide between heart-stopping topwater strikes or bottom-bumping crankbaits based on season and conditions.
blog_posts.content_html:
Topwater vs crankbait: when to choose each for bass

Both topwaters and crankbaits are proven bass producers, but they shine under different conditions. Knowing when to pick one over the other will help you catch more fish and cover water efficiently.

When crankbaits shine

In spring and fall, when bass key on baitfish around rock and wood, crankbaits like the RiverRattler Squarebill 1.5 and CrawChaser Flat-Side Crankbait are hard to beat.

View RiverRattler Squarebill 1.5 | View CrawChaser Flat-Side Crankbait

When topwater takes over

During low-light periods and over shallow vegetation, topwater baits get the nod. Once the sun gets high or fish slide deeper, switch back to lipless and diving crankbaits like the ShadFlash Lipless Crankbait 1/2 oz or DeepCreep Medium Diver.

View ShadFlash Lipless Crankbait 1/2 oz | View DeepCreep Medium Diver Crankbait

Rotate through these options based on water depth, clarity, and fish activity level to stay on the bite all day.

blog_posts.title: Seasonal line selection for trout and bass
blog_posts.excerpt: Match your line type and pound test to the season—fluorocarbon for winter trout, braid for summer grass bass.
blog_posts.content_html:
Seasonal line selection for trout and bass

Choosing the right line type and pound test by season can dramatically improve your presentation. Here’s a seasonal breakdown for trout and bass.

Winter trout: light fluorocarbon

In cold, clear water, go with a dedicated trout fluorocarbon like ClearCreek Trout Fluorocarbon Line in 4–6 lb test.

View ClearCreek Trout Fluorocarbon Line

Spring and summer bass: braid plus leader

When grass grows thick, 40–65 lb braid such as PowerWeave Braided Line 300 yd - 40 lb or PowerWeave Max 65 lb helps you rip baits free and horse big fish out of cover.

View PowerWeave Braided Line 40 lb | View PowerWeave Max 65 lb

Adjust your leader material and length based on water clarity, and you’ll get the best of both worlds: stealth and strength.

blog_posts.title: Holiday gift guide: tackle under $50
blog_posts.excerpt: Great fishing gifts that won’t blow the budget, including tackle boxes, crankbaits, and trout spoons.
blog_posts.content_html:
Holiday gift guide: tackle under $50

Shopping for an angler but not sure what to buy? These budget-friendly picks all come in under $50 and are sure to see plenty of use on the water.

LakePro 3-Tray Tackle Box

A classic hard tackle box with three trays and customizable compartments, perfect for new and experienced anglers alike.

Discounted doesn’t mean ineffective. Baits like the RiverRattler Squarebill 1.5 and ShadFlash Lipless Crankbait 1/2 oz are proven fish-catchers at a lower price.

View RiverRattler Squarebill 1.5 | View ShadFlash Lipless Crankbait 1/2 oz

Trout spoon for the cold months

For the trout angler, a WinterGlow Trout Spoon 1/8 oz makes a thoughtful stocking stuffer that will see plenty of winter use.

View WinterGlow Trout Spoon 1/8 oz

Combine a couple of these items and you’ll have a thoughtful gift bundle while staying within a reasonable budget.

blog_posts.title: Beginner bass spinning setup: rod, reel, and line
blog_posts.excerpt: A simple spinning combo with the right rod power and line will cover most beginner bass techniques.
blog_posts.content_html:
Beginner bass spinning setup: rod, reel, and line

If you’re new to bass fishing, a well-balanced spinning setup is the easiest way to get started. You can fish soft plastics, small crankbaits, and finesse rigs on one combo.

Rod: medium and medium-heavy options

A 6'9" medium-power rod like the EchoLake Bass Spinning Rod 6'9" Medium is perfect for weightless plastics and light jigs. If you want more versatility for heavier baits, step up to the EchoLake Bass Spinning Rod 7'0" Medium-Heavy.

View EchoLake 6'9" Medium Spinning Rod | View EchoLake 7'0" Medium-Heavy Spinning Rod

Line: braid with a leader

Spool your reel with a 20–30 lb braid such as PowerWeave Braided Line 300 yd - 40 lb and add a fluorocarbon leader for clear water. This setup casts well and offers great sensitivity for light bites.

View PowerWeave Braided Line 300 yd - 40 lb

With this basic setup, you’ll have everything you need to learn the fundamentals of bass fishing without overcomplicating your gear.
